event: The Good Samaritan tending to the wounded traveler
symbols: Samaritan bending over the wounded man — compassion overcoming social and ethnic barriers
wounded naked traveler — the vulnerability of the human condition
roadside setting — the dangerous road from Jerusalem to Jericho
act of tending wounds — mercy in action, the embodiment of neighbor-love
distant figure on horseback — possibly the priest or Levite who passed by on the other side
description: A realist painting showing a sturdy, dark-haired man in a vest kneeling over a semi-nude wounded man lying on the ground. The kneeling figure tends to the wounded man's head with gentle, careful attention. The injured man appears unconscious or in pain, with his bare torso exposed and body limp on the rough ground. In the background, a faint figure on horseback is visible in the distance. The landscape is sparse and open, with a warm, hazy sky. The painting has bold, broad brushwork and an earthy, warm palette.
theological_description: The Parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37) is Jesus's definitive answer to the question 'Who is my neighbor?' A man fell among robbers on the road from Jerusalem to Jericho, was stripped, beaten, and left half dead. A priest and a Levite — religious professionals — 'passed by on the other side.' But a Samaritan — despised by Jews as a religious and ethnic outsider — 'was moved with compassion. He went to him and bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine' (Luke 10:33-34). The parable subverts every expectation: the religious insiders fail while the outsider embodies God's love. Jesus concludes by reversing the original question: 'Which of these three proved to be a neighbor?' The answer: 'The one who showed him mercy.' Jesus commands: 'Go, and do likewise' (Luke 10:37). The parable illustrates the second great commandment: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself' (Leviticus 19:18), while radically expanding the definition of 'neighbor' to include everyone, especially the enemy. In Christian typology, the Good Samaritan has been read as Christ himself — the despised one who comes to bind the wounds of fallen humanity, pouring the oil and wine of sacramental grace.

scripture_refs.reason: The Parable of the Good Samaritan — the event directly depicted
The Samaritan binding wounds and pouring oil and wine — the specific moment depicted
Love your neighbor as yourself — the commandment the parable illustrates and expands
What does the Lord require? To do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly — the Samaritan embodies this
As you did it to one of the least of these, you did it to me — the christological dimension of caring for the wounded
